worm is spread under thee, and the worms cover thee. Although the king of Babylon may have enjoyed luxury and wealth during mortality, his dead body, like that of all the deceased, will lie in the dust, and worms will eat his flesh (Job 21:26). Spread or cover suggests a bed, since the king, who sat on his throne in temporal glory, now sleeps in a cold bed in hell; he leaves his pomp on earth,…

KJV: But thou art cast out of thy grave like an abominable branch, [and as] the raiment of those that are slain, thrust through with a sword, that go down to the stones of the pit; as a carcase trodden under feet.

BRANCH, Branch, or branch: nêtser Is Christ the branch (or nêtser) of Isaiah 11:1? There are only four references in the Hebrew Bible that use nêtser for branch. Of course, one is Isaiah 11:1, as shown above. The other three are as follows: Isaiah 14:19: In reference to Lucifer, its reads "But thou art cast out of thy grave like an abominable branch, and as the raiment of those that are slain,…
cast out of thy grave like an abominable branch - his body would remain unburied which anciently was considered to be a great curse - also can represent that he won't even have a body

Hear what the prophet Isaiah says, concerning the children of the wicked; he declares, “The seed of evildoers shall never be renowned. Prepare slaughter for his children, for the iniquity of their fathers ; that they do not rise, nor possess the land, nor fill the face of the world with cities.” (Isa. 14: 20, 21.) Now would it not be far better for them not to marry than to be the means of…
